name: Forgotten Way
name_original: Campo de Santiago y Ermita de Santiago – ida y vuelta desde Fasgar por el Camino Olividado (Reserva de la Biosfera Valles de Omaña y Luna)
description: Hike the moderate 6.4-mile Campo de Santiago y Ermita de Santiago route from Fasgar, gaining 1507 feet through a Biosphere Reserve.
region_intro: Hiking around Murias De Paredes offers access to the western mountains of León, characterized by the Cantabrian mountain range and its diverse natural environment. The region features gentle valleys, significant forest areas including birch and oak groves, and is crisscrossed by rivers such as the Omaña, Salce, and Vallegordo. As part of the Omaña and Luna Valleys Biosphere Reserve, the area provides varied terrain for outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around Murias De Paredes The most popular hiking route is Pico Catoute and Pico de la Cernella – circular via the Laguna de la Rebeza from Colinas del Campo de Martín Moro, a 8.9 miles (14.4 km) trail that takes 6 hours 44 minutes to complete, offering significant elevation gain and mountain views. Another top favourite among local hikers is Laguna de la Rebeza y Campo de Santiago – circular desde Colinas de Campo (Reserva de la Biosfera Valles de Omaña y Luna), a difficult 10.8 miles (17.4 km) path through the Biosphere Reserve, featuring mountain landscapes and a lake. Local hikers also love the Pico Nevadín from Salientes Trail, a 7.6 miles (12.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain towards Pico Nevadín, often completed in about 5 hours 14 minutes. Hiking around Murias De Paredes is defined by the Cantabrian mountain range, extensive birch groves, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Murias De Paredes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from around 40 reviews. More than 450 hikers have used komoot to explore Murias De Paredes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Murias De Paredes?
A: Murias De Paredes offers a wide selection of over 25 hiking trails. These routes cater to various skill levels, with options ranging from easy walks to more challenging mountain ascents.

Q: Are there hiking trails suitable for beginners or easy walks in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, while many routes are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy trail available. The region's gentle valleys and forest areas, such as the Ruta de los Calderones and forests of La Caldanosa, also provide opportunities for pleasant, less strenuous walks.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14987028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brañaronda Waterfall – Ancient Yews of Brañaronda loop from Cerro Mestura&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 6.8 km circular hike. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1041157181/laguna-de-la-rebeza-y-campo-de-santiago-circular-desde-colinas-de-campo-reserva-de-la-biosfera-valles-de-omana-y-luna" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de la Rebeza y Campo de Santiago – circular desde Colinas de Campo&lt;/a&gt;, which is a more difficult 17.4 km route through the Biosphere Reserve.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, the region is home to beautiful waterfalls. The Vivero waterfall, formed by the Omaña river, is a notable natural attraction, and the Cascada de Los Bayos is another nearby option. You can also explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14987028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Brañaronda Waterfall – Ancient Yews of Brañaronda loop from Cerro Mestura&lt;/a&gt; for a hike that includes a waterfall.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on hikes in Murias De Paredes?
A: Hikers can expect diverse natural features, including the impressive Cantabrian mountain range with peaks like Pico Catoute and Pico Nevadín, extensive birch and oak forests, and rivers such as the Omaña, Salce, and Vallegordo. The area is also part of the Omaña and Luna Valleys Biosphere Reserve, known for its rich biodiversity.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or historical sites to see while hiking?
A: Beyond natural beauty, the area offers historical attractions. You can find the medieval bridge of 'La Lechería,' ancient mills, and traditional stone architecture. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518472" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Campo de Martín Moro Toledano&lt;/a&gt; is a notable religious building, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2611232" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palace of Quiñones&lt;/a&gt; is another historical site worth exploring.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Murias De Paredes?
A: The region offers hiking opportunities throughout the year. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes, while summer is ideal for higher-altitude routes. Even in winter, the Vivero waterfall can freeze, creating an impressive ice spectacle, though some routes may require specific gear.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Murias De Paredes?
A: The hiking routes in Murias De Paredes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Hikers often praise the stunning mountain views from peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Catoute (2,117 m)&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5555072" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Nevadín (2,077 m)&lt;/a&gt;, the tranquility of the extensive forests, and the diverse natural environment of the Biosphere Reserve.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting in Murias De Paredes?
A: Yes, as part of the Omaña and Luna Valleys Biosphere Reserve, the region is rich in biodiversity. It is home to endemic species such as the Cantabrian brown bear and the capercaillie, offering opportunities for wildlife observation, especially in quieter forest areas.

Q: Is there a greenway or accessible path for easier outdoor activities?
A: Yes, the Vía Verde del Ferrocarril Ponferrada–Villablino, a former railway line, has been converted into a greenway. A pleasant section between Villablino and Villaseca de Laciana is popular for outdoor sports and features picnic areas, offering an accessible option for walks or cycling.

Q: Are there any routes that offer panoramic views of the surrounding mountains?
A: Absolutely. Hikes to peaks like Pico Catoute and Pico Nevadín provide breathtaking 360-degree views of mountain ranges such as Catoute, Cernella, Valdeiglesias, and Tambarón. The 'Fuentes del Omaña' Route also offers impressive panoramic views from various mountain passes as it ascends towards the source of the Omaña River.

Old abandoned dairy known as the "Donato's house" located in a beautiful setting next to the Boeza river.

Starting from the town of Colinas del Campo or the town of Fasgar, the Hermitage of Campo de Martín Moro Toledano is an ideal destination to complement our route through Campo de Santiago. A wide meadow surrounded by mountains that offers a beautiful picture.

faqs.question: Where does the trail to Campo de Santiago and Ermita de Santiago start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail officially begins in the village of Fasgar. You'll find parking available within or near the village, which serves as the primary trailhead for this return trip via the Camino Olvidado.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this hike?
faqs.answer: Komoot rates this return trip as a 'moderate' hike, requiring good fitness. While some sources might describe sections of the longer Camino Olvidado as 'high' difficulty due to significant elevation changes and rough terrain, this specific route from Fasgar is manageable for hikers with a reasonable level of experience and stamina.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter diverse terrain, starting with an ascent from Fasgar. The path winds through forests of mountain ash, birch, holly, and yew trees. You'll also follow the winding course of the Boeza River, crossing it multiple times, and traverse the glacial valley of Campo de Santiago. Expect a mix of forest paths, rocky sections, and open mountain trails.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in natural beauty and historical significance. Key highlights include the Campo de Sanabria, the historic Casa de Donato, and the Hermitage of Campo de Martín Moro Toledano. The trail also offers stunning views of the Sierra de Gistredo, including peaks like Catoute and Tambarón, and the imposing glacial Vega that forms the Campo de Santiago valley.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, as it passes through the Biosphere Reserve Valles de Omaña y Luna, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and respect any livestock you might encounter.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to hike in the Biosphere Reserve Valles de Omaña y Luna?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a specific permit or to pay an entrance fee to hike this trail within the Biosphere Reserve Valles de Omaña y Luna. The reserve is open for public enjoyment, but always remember to practice Leave No Trace principles to preserve its natural beauty.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this route is typically from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally more favorable, and the natural scenery, including the diverse flora of the Biosphere Reserve, is at its most vibrant. Avoid winter due to potential snow and colder conditions at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route largely follows a significant portion (approximately 90%) of the historic Camino Olvidado (Cirujales-Cacabelos). The Camino Olvidado is an ancient pilgrimage route to Santiago de Compostela, offering a quieter and more remote experience than the more popular French Way.

faqs.question: What makes the Camino Olvidado section of this trail unique?
faqs.answer: The Camino Olvidado, or 'Forgotten Way,' offers a unique, authentic, and less crowded pilgrimage or hiking experience. It's one of the earliest documented routes to Santiago de Compostela, predating the French Way, and provides a glimpse into what the Camino might have felt like decades ago, with sparse services and a strong connection to nature and history.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife and plants might I see in the Biosphere Reserve Valles de Omaña y Luna?
faqs.answer: The Biosphere Reserve is known for its immense biological diversity, marking a transition between two climate types. You might spot indigenous Iberian species and pass through forests of birch, willow, alder, poplar, and oak. Keep an eye out for various bird species and other local fauna, though direct sightings of larger wildlife can be rare.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or services available along the trail?
faqs.answer: Services along the Camino Olvidado, including this section, can be sparse. Fasgar, your starting point, is a small village. It's advisable to carry sufficient water, snacks, and any other essentials you might need for the duration of your hike, as there are long stretches with no services.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of Campo de Martín Moro Toledano
